candidates.item: Every connector is a declarative YAML file in Git — reviewable in PRs, testable locally, reproducible across environments.
candidates.why_interesting: Most iPaaS and integration platforms hide connector logic in a proprietary GUI or black-box runtime. StackOne externalises every connector as a plain YAML file you can diff, review in a PR, and test locally — treating integrations like software code. That's a genuinely distinctive engineering stance, not a generic feature.
candidates.recency_note: Confirmed on current homepage and Falcon engine page as of search results dated April 2026.

candidates.item: Falcon can reduce tool definitions from hundreds to single digits, cutting token usage by 40–60%.
candidates.why_interesting: This is a concrete, quantified mechanism — not a vague 'optimises context' claim. When 20 MCP servers worth of tool definitions can consume ~138 k tokens before an agent reasons at all, a 40–60% reduction is a meaningful engineering result worth reacting to.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced directly from the Falcon engine page, dated April 14 2026 in search results.

candidates.item: With Falcon, you flag any action for approval in a single YAML config line. Classify actions by risk level — auto-approve reads, require human approval for writes, deletes, or anything your policy flags.
candidates.why_interesting: Human-in-the-loop approval for AI agents is a hot but rarely-implemented feature. The fact that it's configured in one YAML line — not a separate workflow tool or a code change — is a specific, surprising design choice.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced from the Falcon engine FAQ page, dated April 14 2026.

candidates.item: Open source prompt injection protection for Agents calling tools (via MCP, CLI or direct function calling). Detect and defend against prompt injection attacks. 22MB, CPU-only, < 10ms latency.
candidates.why_interesting: StackOne shipped Defender as a standalone open-source npm/Python package — a 22 MB int8-quantised ONNX model that runs fully offline with no external API calls. The comparison point they publish is striking: 22 MB vs 1,064 MB for Meta Prompt Guard v1, at ~4 ms vs 43 ms, with a 5.8% false positive rate vs 49.9%. That's a real, independently-verifiable engineering result, not a marketing claim.
candidates.recency_note: GitHub repo confirmed active as of May 15 2026; benchmark comparison page at stackone.com/platform/prompt-injection-guard/ updated March 2026.

candidates.item: We ran this exact attack against Claude 4.5 Sonnet with real Gmail MCP tools. Without the defense layer, Claude 4.5 Sonnet happily forwarded the email summary to the external address. With @stackone/defender enabled, the exfiltration was blocked before the agent saw the content.
candidates.why_interesting: This is a concrete live demonstration — not a theoretical security claim. Running an actual data-exfiltration prompt injection against Claude 4.5 Sonnet via Gmail MCP and publishing the before/after result is unusually blunt and specific; it makes the risk real and the product proof tangible.
candidates.recency_note: Blog post dated March 12 2026 per search metadata.

candidates.item: The StackOne Agent is an AI assistant that creates complete StackOne Connector configurations. It can run inside a coding agent (Claude Code preferred, Cursor or others supported) and is also available as a standalone agent (no separate coding agent subscription required when used standalone). It leverages 20+ specialised MCP tools to research APIs, generate and validate YAML, and exhaustively test actions using the Falcon execution engine.
candidates.why_interesting: The builder is itself an MCP-native AI agent — it uses 20+ specialised MCP tools internally to do its job. Most 'AI-assisted integration' tools generate code snippets you still have to wire up; this one auto-discovers docs, generates structured YAML, and validates against the live API in a single conversation, then produces PR-ready files with secrets auto-scrubbed.
candidates.recency_note: Docs page confirmed in search results; builder page at stackone.com/platform/ai-integration-builder/ dated April 14 2026.

candidates.item: Compliance leader Drata recently used the platform to rapidly launch 100 integrations, while management development leader Mindtools delivered a Workday integration in record time, securing a major contract.
candidates.why_interesting: Two real, named customer outcomes with concrete stakes: 100 integrations launched fast (Drata), and a Workday integration that won a 'major contract' (Mindtools). These are specific business results, not generic 'our customers love us' claims.
candidates.recency_note: From the May 6 2025 Series A press release, confirmed across multiple outlets.

candidates.item: The company's investors include Google Ventures, Workday Ventures, XTX Ventures, Episode 1, Playfair, as well as the founders of GitHub, Onfido, and Hofy.
candidates.why_interesting: The GitHub founder as an angel investor is a genuinely surprising and signal-rich detail — GitHub is the canonical example of developer infrastructure becoming a category-defining platform. That its founder bet on StackOne is a striking endorsement for a dev-infrastructure play. Workday Ventures also investing (a direct enterprise SaaS incumbent) adds a different dimension.
candidates.recency_note: Confirmed in Series A press release, May 6 2025, and in multiple independent outlets.

candidates.item: Having recently surpassed 1 billion API calls, and with customers including Drata, Attensi, Localyze and more across three continents.
candidates.why_interesting: 1 billion API calls is a hard scale signal for a company founded in 2023 — it's the kind of number that makes the 'this is infrastructure not just a product' claim credible. Most 2-year-old integration startups don't have a billion-call proof point.
candidates.recency_note: Cited in Series A announcement, May 2025. May be higher by now but no later figure found.

candidates.item: When you connect 20 MCP servers to an agent, tool definitions alone consume roughly 138,000 tokens.
candidates.why_interesting: This is a concrete, non-obvious problem statement StackOne publishes as original research — quantifying exactly why naive MCP deployment breaks at scale. It makes the token-optimization work feel necessary rather than nice-to-have, and positions StackOne as a company that has done the maths others haven't published.
candidates.recency_note: Blog post dated February 17 2026 per search metadata.
